summary: O'Neil recounts her two-day jury duty experience, focusing on a comedic incident involving an unattended phone's repeated Amber Alerts. A particularly loud and hostile woman spent an hour complaining about the annoying noise, demanding someone turn it off, while becoming increasingly agitated. The twist: after nearly an hour of complaints, someone finally discovered the offending phone belonged to the loud woman herself. The post is a character study in irony and human behavior under the tedium of civic duty.
key_points: Jury duty creates a peculiar social pressure cooker where boredom amplifies small annoyances into major incidents
The clerk Bill exemplifies patient professionalism in the face of difficult people, described as 'a saint'
The loud woman's hostility throughout both days foreshadowed her role as the unwitting source of her own complaint
Group dynamics in forced waiting situations create contagious reactions - both frustration and laughter
The story serves as a perfect illustration of projection and lack of self-awareness
money_quotes: It's like you're on a two day plane ride to nowhere.
Being prepared to do it was surely enough.
The guy next to me kept mumbling, 'I gotta get outta here' under his breath, while shaking his leg furiously.
This time the loud woman added some sarcastic comments about how SOME people seemed to think her suffering was FUNNY, which made me simultaneously laugh harder and consider suicide.
key_insight: The person most loudly complaining about a problem is sometimes the one causing it, a lesson in self-awareness delivered through perfect comedic timing.
